 {"id":"caam-4eg","title":"Browser-Profile Aware Login","description":"## Overview
The PRIMARY pain point for users with multiple 'all you can eat' subscriptions is the OAuth login flow. When you have 5 Claude Max accounts (each with a different Google account), the browser OAuth flow is painful because:
1. Browser opens with your 'default' Google account
2. You have to click 'switch account'
3. Find the right account from the list
4. Then authorize

This takes 30-60 seconds and is error-prone (logging into wrong account).

## Solution
Associate each caam profile with a specific browser profile (e.g., Chrome profile). When login flows need a browser, open the URL in THAT browser profile, which already has the correct Google account logged in.

## Key Insight
Chrome (and Firefox) support launching with a specific profile directory. This is the same mechanism that allows people to have separate 'work' and 'personal' browser profiles. We leverage this to make OAuth flows automatically use the right Google account.

## Implementation Approach
1. Add browser profile config to Profile struct (browser_command, browser_profile_dir)
2. Create browser launcher abstraction
3. Detect localhost redirect URLs from CLI stdout
4. Launch browser with correct profile when URL detected
5. Support Chrome, Firefox, and 'default browser' fallback

## User Value
Reduces login friction from 30-60 seconds to ~5 seconds (just click authorize). Eliminates risk of logging into wrong account.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-17T00:49:43.32942877-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-17T01:22:09.970282351-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-17T01:22:09.970282351-05:00","close_reason":"Browser-Profile Aware Login epic complete.  {"id":"caam-4ve","title":"TUI hot reload integration","description":"## Purpose
Integrate the file system watcher with the TUI to automatically refresh the profile
list when files change on disk.

## Background
This enables a seamless workflow where:
1. User has TUI open in Terminal 1
2. User runs `caam backup claude newprofile` in Terminal 2
3. TUI automatically shows the new profile without restart

codex-pool achieves similar with `POST /admin/reload`. For caam, we use file watching.

## User Experience
```
# Terminal 1: TUI running
┌─ Claude Profiles ─────────────────────┐
│ ● work@company.com    🟢 Active       │
│   personal@gmail.com  🟡              │
└───────────────────────────────────────┘

# Terminal 2: Add new profile
$ caam backup claude newproject@corp.com
Profile saved.

# Terminal 1: TUI auto-updates (no restart needed!)
┌─ Claude Profiles ─────────────────────┐
│ ● work@company.com       🟢 Active    │
│   personal@gmail.com     🟡           │
│   newproject@corp.com    🟢 NEW       │  ← appeared automatically
└───────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Technical Implementation

### Message Type
```go
// internal/tui/messages.go
type profilesChangedMsg struct {
    event watcher.Event
}
```

### Model Integration
```go
// internal/tui/model.go
type Model struct {
    // ... existing fields
    watcher *watcher.Watcher
}

// Init starts the file watcher
func (m Model) Init() tea.Cmd {
    return tea.Batch(
        m.loadProfiles(),
        m.watchProfiles(),
    )
}

// watchProfiles returns a command that listens for file changes
func (m Model) watchProfiles() tea.Cmd {
    return func() tea.Msg {
        select {
        case event := <-m.watcher.Events():
            return profilesChangedMsg{event: event}
        case err := <-m.watcher.Errors():
            return errMsg{err: err}
        }
    }
}

// Update handles the change event
func (m Model) Update(msg tea.Msg) (tea.Model, tea.Cmd) {
    switch msg := msg.(type) {
    case profilesChangedMsg:
        // Show brief notification
        m.statusMsg = fmt.Sprintf("Profile %s %s", 
            msg.event.Profile, 
            eventTypeVerb(msg.event.Type))
        
        // Reload profiles and continue watching
        return m, tea.Batch(
            m.loadProfiles(),
            m.watchProfiles(),
        )
    }
}
```

### Visual Feedback
```go
// Show "NEW" badge briefly for added profiles
type profileBadge struct {
    profile  string
    badge    string
    expiry   time.Time
}

func (m *Model) addBadge(profile, badge string, duration time.Duration) {
    m.badges[profile] = profileBadge{
        profile: profile,
        badge:   badge,
        expiry:  time.Now().Add(duration),
    }
}
```

### Graceful Degradation
```go
// If watcher fails, TUI still works but without auto-refresh
func (m Model) Init() tea.Cmd {
    cmds := []tea.Cmd{m.loadProfiles()}
    
    w, err := watcher.New(m.profilesDir)
    if err != nil {
        // Log warning but continue without watching
        log.Printf("Warning: file watching unavailable: %v", err)
    } else {
        m.watcher = w
        cmds = append(cmds, m.watchProfiles())
    }
    
    return tea.Batch(cmds...)
}
```

## Files to Modify
- internal/tui/model.go
- internal/tui/messages.go
- internal/tui/profiles_panel.go (for badges)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] New profiles appear automatically in TUI
- [ ] Modified profiles trigger refresh
- [ ] Deleted profiles are removed from list
- [ ] Brief notification shows what changed
- [ ] NEW badge shown for 5 seconds on additions
- [ ] No crash if watcher is unavailable
- [ ] Clean shutdown stops watcher","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-17T16:24:29.724093683-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-17T19:41:51.401036395-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-17T19:41:51.401036395-05:00","close_reason":"Wired profile watcher into TUI for hot reload; load vault profiles, show NEW badge, and cleanly close watcher.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"caam-4ve","depends_on_id":"caam-0gx","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-17T16:29:25.420647662-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}"}],"comments":[{"id":13,"issue_id":"caam-4ve","author":"ubuntu","text":"Starting TUI hot reload integration: wire internal/watcher into Bubble Tea model to reload vault profile list on fs events, show brief status + NEW badge on additions, and ensure watcher closes cleanly on quit. (Agent mail not configured in this env; using issue comments.)","created_at":"2025-12-18T00:32:52Z"},{"id":14,"issue_id":"caam-4ve","author":"ubuntu","text":"Implemented hot reload in TUI: added fsnotify watcher on vault dir, Bubble Tea cmds/messages to reload profiles on add/modify/delete, vault-backed loadProfiles (with active profile detection), and 5s NEW badge on additions w/ expiry.  {"id":"caam-9os","title":"OAuth refresh handler for Google Gemini","description":"# OAuth Refresh Handler for Google Gemini

## Purpose
Implement OAuth token refresh for Google Gemini using standard Google OAuth2 flow.

## Reference (from codex-pool)
```go
func (h *proxyHandler) refreshGeminiAccount(ctx context.Context, a *Account, 
    refreshTok string) error {
    form := url.Values{}
    form.Set("client_id", geminiOAuthClientID())
    form.Set("grant_type", "refresh_token")
    form.Set("refresh_token", refreshTok)
    // POST to https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token
}
```

**KEY DIFFERENCE**: Gemini uses form-encoded body, NOT JSON!

## Technical Requirements

### Constants
```go
const (
    GeminiTokenURL = "https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token"
)

// Client ID from gcloud ADC or gemini CLI
func geminiClientID() string {
    // This may need to be read from the ADC file
    // or use a known client ID
}
```

### Auth File Location
```
~/.config/gcloud/application_default_credentials.json
```

### ADC File Format
```json
{
  "client_id": "764086051850-6qr4p6gpi6hn506pt8ejuq83di341hur.apps.googleusercontent.com",
  "client_secret": "d-FL95Q19q7MQmFpd7hHD0Ty",
  "refresh_token": "1//...",
  "type": "authorized_user"
}
```

**Note**: ADC file contains client_id and client_secret - use these for refresh!

### Refresh Function
```go
func RefreshGeminiToken(ctx context.Context, clientID, clientSecret, refreshToken string) (*TokenResponse, error) {
    form := url.Values{}
    form.Set("client_id", clientID)
    form.Set("client_secret", clientSecret)
    form.Set("grant_type", "refresh_token")
    form.Set("refresh_token", refreshToken)
    
    req, err := http.NewRequestWithContext(ctx, "POST", GeminiTokenURL, 
        strings.NewReader(form.Encode()))
    req.Header.Set("Content-Type", "application/x-www-form-urlencoded")
    
    resp, err := http.DefaultClient.Do(req)
    if err != nil {
        return nil, fmt.Errorf("gemini refresh failed: %w", err)
    }
    defer resp.Body.Close()
    
    if resp.StatusCode != 200 {
        body, _ := io.ReadAll(resp.Body)
        return nil, fmt.Errorf("gemini refresh error %d: %s", 
            resp.StatusCode, string(body))
    }
    
    var tokenResp TokenResponse
    json.NewDecoder(resp.Body).Decode(&tokenResp)
    
    return &tokenResp, nil
}
```

### Google TokenResponse
```go
// Google returns slightly different format
type GoogleTokenResponse struct {
    AccessToken  string `json:"access_token"`
    ExpiresIn    int    `json:"expires_in"`    // Seconds
    Scope        string `json:"scope"`
    TokenType    string `json:"token_type"`
    // Note: refresh_token is NOT returned on refresh
}
```

### File Update Approach
For Gemini/gcloud ADC, we typically do NOT update the ADC file. Instead:
1. Store the access token separately for caam use
2. Or update health metadata with expiry only
3. The ADC file is managed by gcloud CLI

```go
func UpdateGeminiHealth(provider, profile string, resp *TokenResponse) error {
    // Update health metadata only
    health := healthStore.GetProfile(provider, profile)
    health.TokenExpiresAt = time.Now().Add(
        time.Duration(resp.ExpiresIn) * time.Second)
    return healthStore.UpdateProfile(provider, profile, health)
}
```

## Alternative: Read-Only Approach
Since gcloud manages ADC, we might just:
1. Read expiry from access token (if JWT)
2. Parse token to check expiry
3. Recommend user run `gcloud auth login` if expired

 {"id":"caam-ah4","title":"Auth file watcher with auto-backup","description":"## Problem
If user logs in directly with a tool (not through caam), they might:
1. Overwrite auth without backing up
2. Lose their only saved copy
3. Not realize caam state is stale

## Solution
Background file watcher that detects auth changes.

## Behavior
When auth files change (outside of caam operations):

```
⚠️ Your Claude auth was modified externally.
   
   Old: user@example.com (saved as 'work')
   New: other@gmail.com (not saved)
   
   Options:
   [S]ave as new profile
   [R]eplace existing 'work'
   [I]gnore (don't track)
   
   Choice [S/r/i]: s
   Profile name: personal
   ✓ Saved claude/personal
```

## Implementation
1. Use existing watcher package (internal/watcher)
2. Monitor auth file paths for all tools
3. Compare content hash to detect real changes
4. Prompt or notify based on config

## Configuration
```yaml
safety:
  watch_auth_files: true
  on_external_change: prompt  # prompt | auto_backup | ignore
  backup_name_pattern: "_captured_%timestamp%"
```

## Modes
- **Interactive (TUI running)**: Show dialog
- **Background daemon**: Desktop notification + auto-backup
- **CLI**: Check on next caam command, offer to save

## Benefits
- Never lose unsaved auth
- Keeps caam state accurate
- Reduces manual backup discipline","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-12-19T19:01:51.658259845-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-19T21:38:06.68369756-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-19T21:38:06.68369756-05:00","close_reason":"Auth file watcher package with change detection, profile matching, and tests","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"caam-ah4","depends_on_id":"caam-m9g","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-19T19:03:43.662065745-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}"}]}
 {"id":"caam-ai6","title":"Research Gemini CLI headless authentication options","description":"# Research Gemini CLI Headless Auth

## Research Findings (Completed)

### Available Methods

#### 1. Device Code Flow (Recent Update - August 2025)
Gemini CLI now provides an auth URL to copy/paste, returning an activation code. This is similar to device code flow:
- CLI shows auth URL
- User opens URL on any device
- User gets code to enter back in CLI
- Works in headless environments!

**Status**: This appears to be a recent improvement. Need to verify current behavior.

#### 2. GEMINI_API_KEY Environment Variable
```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-api-key"
gemini "your prompt"
```
Direct API key authentication for Gemini API.

#### 3. GOOGLE_API_KEY Environment Variable
```bash
export GOOGLE_API_KEY="your-google-api-key"
gemini "your prompt"
```
Google Cloud API key authentication.

#### 4. Application Default Credentials (ADC)
```bash
export GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS="/path/to/service-account.json"
export GOOGLE_CLOUD_PROJECT="your-project"
export GOOGLE_CLOUD_LOCATION="us-central1"
gemini "your prompt"
```
For service account authentication - best for automation and CI/CD.

#### 5. BROWSER Workaround (Legacy)
Set BROWSER to a script that captures the auth URL:
```bash
#!/bin/bash
echo "Auth URL: \$1" >> /tmp/gemini-auth-url.txt
```
Then manually complete auth on another device.

### Auth Files Location

~/.gemini/
├── google_accounts.json  # Active account email
├── oauth_creds.json      # OAuth credentials  
├── settings.json         # Auth type selection
├── installation_id       # Installation identifier
└── state.json            # Session state

### Implementation Path for caam

1. **Verify Device Code Flow**: Test if current gemini CLI supports URL-based auth
2. **AuthModeDeviceCode**: If supported, add to Gemini provider
3. **AuthModeAPIKey**: Support GEMINI_API_KEY and GOOGLE_API_KEY
4. **AuthModeServiceAccount**: Support ADC for automation

 {"id":"caam-g2yz.1","title":"Profile Description Field","description":"## Overview
Add a `Description` field to the Profile struct for free-form notes about what each profile is used for.

## Problem Statement
Users with multiple profiles struggle to remember what each profile is for. The current `AccountLabel` field is semantic for email/account identification, not purpose descriptions. Users need a place for notes like:
- "Client X project"
- "Free tier for testing"
- "Team shared account - billing goes to ops@company.com"
- "Personal pro account - don't use for client work"

## Design Decisions

### Single Field vs Multiple Fields
**Decision:** Single `Description` string field
**Rationale:** Keeping it simple. No need for separate "short" and "long" descriptions. Users can write whatever they want. If they need structure, they can use their own conventions.

### Field Properties
- **Type:** `Description string`
- **JSON tag:** `json:"description,omitempty"`
- **omitempty:** Yes - no migration needed for existing profiles
- **Max length:** Recommended 200 chars, not enforced (user responsibility)
- **Validation:** None - free-form text, trust the user

### Display Contexts
Description appears in:
1. `caam ls` - truncated to ~40 chars if long, with "..." indicator
2. `caam status` - shown when relevant profile is active
3. `caam profile show <tool> <profile>` - full description
4. TUI profile list - truncated to fit column
5. TUI profile detail panel - full description

## Implementation Tasks

### 1. Profile Struct Enhancement (internal/profile/profile.go)
```go
type Profile struct {
    // ... existing fields ...
    Description string `json:"description,omitempty"` // Free-form notes
}
```

### 2. CLI: Add Flag to `caam add` and `caam profile add`
- Add `--description/-d` flag
- Example: `caam add claude --name work --description "Client consulting projects"`

### 3. CLI: Create `caam profile describe` Command
```
caam profile describe <tool> <profile> [description]
```
- If description provided: set it
- If no description: show current description (or prompt interactively?)
- Add `--clear` flag to remove description

### 4. Update Display Commands
- `caam ls`: Add description column (truncated)
- `caam profile show`: Include description in output
- `caam status`: Show description of active profile

### 5. TUI Integration
- Profile list: Show description in secondary text
- Profile detail: Display full description
- Add description editing capability (future enhancement, not MVP)

## Testing Strategy
- Unit tests for Profile serialization with description
- Integration test for describe command
- Test truncation logic for display
- Test backward compatibility (profiles without description)

## Backward Compatibility
Fully backward compatible - existing profiles without description field work unchanged due to omitempty tag.

- cmd/caam/cmd/list.go - Update output formatting
- cmd/caam/cmd/status.go - Show description
- internal/tui/profiles.go - Display in list and detail

This feature adds significant complexity while favorites already provide profile organization. Consider this a "nice to have" after core features are solid.

## Problem Statement
As profile counts grow, users need ways to categorize and filter profiles beyond simple listing. Tags enable organizational views by project, team, environment, client, etc.

## Key Distinction from Favorites
- **Favorites:** Ordered priority list (1st favorite, 2nd favorite, etc.)
- **Tags:** Unordered categories (work, personal, project-x, testing)

However, the overlap is significant. Users with 5-10 profiles may not need both systems.

## Design Decisions

### Tag Format
**Decision:** Simple string tags (not key:value pairs)
**Constraints:**
- Lowercase, alphanumeric + hyphens only
- Max 32 characters per tag
- Max 10 tags per profile

### Storage
```go
type Profile struct {
    Tags []string \`json:"tags,omitempty"\`
}
```

1. Add Tags field to Profile struct
2. Create `caam tag add/remove/list` commands (or `caam profile tag ...`)
3. Add `--tag` filter to `caam ls`
4. TUI integration

## Recommendation
Defer this until users explicitly request it. ## Overview

### 1. Burn Rate Calculation Tests
```go
func TestBurnRateCalculation(t *testing.T) {
    tests := []struct {
        name           string
        entries        []*logs.LogEntry
        window         time.Duration
        wantTokens     float64
        wantConfidence float64
    }{
        {
            name: "steady burn rate",
            entries: generateSteadyUsage(1000, 10, time.Hour), // 1000 tokens each, 10 entries over 1 hour
            window: time.Hour,
            wantTokens: 10000.0 / 60, // ~167 tokens/min
            wantConfidence: 0.9,       // High confidence (steady)
        },
        {
            name: "variable burn rate",
            entries: generateVariableUsage(...),
            wantConfidence: 0.5, // Lower confidence (variable)
        },
        {
            name: "no recent data",
            entries: nil,
            wantConfidence: 0.0,
        },
    }
    
    for _, tc := range tests {
        t.Run(tc.name, func(t *testing.T) {
            t.Logf("[TEST] Input: %d entries over %v", len(tc.entries), tc.window)
            
            rate := prediction.CalculateBurnRate(tc.entries, tc.window)
            
            t.Logf("[TEST] Result: %.2f tokens/min, confidence=%.2f", 
                rate.TokensPerMinute, rate.Confidence)
            
            assertApprox(t, tc.wantTokens, rate.TokensPerMinute, 0.1)
            assertApprox(t, tc.wantConfidence, rate.Confidence, 0.1)
        })
    }
}
```

### 2. Depletion Prediction Tests
- Predict depletion with known burn rate
- Predict with zero burn rate (infinite time)
- Predict when already at 100%
- Predict with window reset before depletion

### 3. Warning Level Tests
```go
func TestWarningLevels(t *testing.T) {
    tests := []struct {
        timeUntil   time.Duration
        wantWarning WarningLevel
    }{
        {2 * time.Hour, WarningNone},
        {25 * time.Minute, WarningApproaching},
        {5 * time.Minute, WarningImminent},
        {0, WarningImminent},
    }
    
    for _, tc := range tests {
        t.Run(tc.timeUntil.String(), func(t *testing.T) {
            pred := &Prediction{
                PredictedTime: time.Now().Add(tc.timeUntil),
            }
            t.Logf("[TEST] Time until depletion: %v", tc.timeUntil)
            t.Logf("[TEST] Expected warning: %v", tc.wantWarning)
            
            warning := pred.GetWarningLevel()
            t.Logf("[TEST] Actual warning: %v", warning)
            
            assert.Equal(t, tc.wantWarning, warning)
        })
    }
}
```

### 4. Alert Generation Tests
- Generate alert when approaching limit
- No duplicate alerts for same condition
- Clear alert when condition resolves
- Multiple profiles with different alerts

### 5. Confidence Calculation Tests
- High sample size = high confidence
- Low variance = high confidence
- Recent data = higher weight
- Stale data = lower confidence

 {"id":"caam-gir","title":"Runtime Enhancements","description":"# Runtime Enhancements

## Purpose
Improve runtime behavior with hot reload, file watching, and signal handling for a smoother user experience.

## Background (from codex-pool)
codex-pool supports hot reload without restart:
```go
POST /admin/reload → h.pool.replace(newAccounts)
```

## Why This Matters
Users often:
1. Run TUI in one terminal
2. Add/modify profiles in another terminal
3. Expect TUI to reflect changes

Without hot reload, users must restart TUI to see changes.

## What This Epic Delivers
1. File watching for profiles directory (fsnotify)
2. Automatic TUI refresh on changes
3. `caam profiles reload` CLI command
4. SIGHUP handling for daemon mode
5. Graceful reload (no state loss)

## Technical Approach

### File Watching
Use fsnotify to watch:
- `~/.caam/profiles/` directory
- Provider-specific auth directories (optional)

Events to handle:
- CREATE: New profile added
- MODIFY: Profile updated
- DELETE: Profile removed
- RENAME: Profile renamed

### TUI Integration
```go
func (m *Model) watchProfiles() {
    watcher, _ := fsnotify.NewWatcher()
    watcher.Add(profilesDir)
    
    for event := range watcher.Events {
        m.reloadProfiles()  // async refresh
    }
}
```

### Signal Handling
```go
sigChan := make(chan os.Signal, 1)
signal.Notify(sigChan, syscall.SIGHUP)

go func() {
    for range sigChan {
        reloadConfiguration()
    }
}()
```

## User Experience
```
# Terminal 1: TUI running
┌─ Claude Profiles ─────────┐
│ ● work@company.com   🟢   │
│   personal@gmail.com 🟡   │
└───────────────────────────┘

# Terminal 2: Add new profile
$ caam backup claude newproject@corp.com
Profile saved.

# Terminal 1: Auto-updates!
┌─ Claude Profiles ─────────┐
│ ● work@company.com   🟢   │
│   personal@gmail.com 🟡   │
│   newproject@corp.com 🟢  │  ← NEW
└───────────────────────────┘
```

## Edge Cases
- Rapid file changes: Debounce (100ms)
- Corrupted profile: Log warning, skip
- Permission denied: Log error, continue watching
- Watch limit exceeded: Fall back to polling

## Dependencies

## Context
Expose export and import functionality in the TUI for users who prefer the graphical interface.

## Implementation

### Export Action

#### Trigger
Press 'E' (shift+e) from main view

#### Flow
1. Show confirmation dialog: "Export vault to zip bundle?"
2. On confirm:
   - Show export options (checkboxes for include-config, include-projects, etc.)
   - Or use sensible defaults and just show progress
3. Execute export to default location (current directory or ~/Downloads?)
4. Show success with path: "Exported to: ~/Exported_Coding_Agent_Account_Auth_Info__As_of__12_19_2025__02_29_PM.zip"

#### Alternative: Simpler Flow
Just export with defaults, no options dialog:
1. Press 'E'
2. Confirm: "Export all profiles to current directory?"
3. Export runs with progress indicator
4. Success message shows path

### Import Action

#### Trigger
Press 'I' (shift+i) from main view

#### Flow
1. Show file path input dialog: "Enter path to bundle zip file:"
   - Or: file browser component (more complex)
2. Validate path exists
3. Parse bundle, show preview:
   ```
   Import Preview:
   - 3 new profiles (alice@gmail.com, bob@gmail.com, work@company.com)
   - 2 existing profiles (will skip)
   Import now? (y/n)
   ```
4. On confirm, execute import
5. Show success message
6. Refresh profile list

- Submit with Enter
- Styling consistent with existing TUI

#### File Path Input

### Key Bindings
- 'E' - Export vault
- 'I' - Import bundle

Add to help panel.

 {"id":"caam-m9g","title":"EPIC: Just Works UX - Zero-friction account switching","description":"## Vision
Transform CAAM from "useful CLI tool" to "invisible magic that just works."

## Success Criteria
- New user productive in <2 minutes (vs current 10+ minutes)
- Daily use requires zero caam commands (shell wrapper handles everything)
- Rate limit recovery is automatic (detect, cooldown, switch, retry)
- Profile management is intuitive (aliases, workspaces, fuzzy matching)

## Core Features (Priority Order)

### P0 - Foundation (Must complete first)
- [x] **caam-q9f**: Wrapper Mode - `caam run <tool>` with auto rate-limit detection and retry
- [x] **caam-bxq**: Init Wizard - Guided setup with auth discovery

### P1 - Shell Integration (Enables invisible UX)
- [ ] **caam-k9z**: Shell integration - aliases, completion, prompt, auto-activate
- [ ] **caam-hxk**: `caam next` - One-command profile rotation

### P2 - Power Features
- [ ] **caam-08w**: Profile aliases and fuzzy matching
- [ ] **caam-t5p**: Profile workspaces - Switch all tools at once
- [ ] **caam-lr2**: `caam add` - One-command account capture
- [ ] **caam-ah4**: Auth file watcher with auto-backup

### P3 - Polish
- [ ] **caam-vog**: `caam verify` - Validate all profile tokens
- [ ] **caam-0l0**: Background daemon for auto-refresh and sync

## Dependency Graph
```
                    ┌─────────────────┐
                    │   caam-m9g      │
                    │  (this epic)    │
                    └────────┬────────┘
                             │
        ┌────────────────────┼────────────────────┐
        │                    │                    │
        ▼                    ▼                    ▼
   ┌─────────┐         ┌─────────┐         ┌─────────┐
   │caam-q9f │         │caam-bxq │         │caam-hxk │
   │ wrapper │         │  init   │         │  next   │
   └────┬────┘         └─────────┘         └─────────┘
        │
        ▼
   ┌─────────┐
   │caam-k9z │ (shell integration uses wrapper)
   │  shell  │
   └─────────┘
```

## Design Principles
1. **Invisible by default** - Works without thinking about it
2. **Progressive disclosure** - Simple commands, advanced options available
3. **Safe by default** - Auto-backup, no data loss
4. **Fast feedback** - Clear status, quick operations

## Anti-patterns to Avoid
- Requiring users to understand internal architecture
- Multi-step workflows for common operations
- Silent failures or unclear error messages
- Configuration complexity

## What "Just Works" Means

**Before (current state):**
```bash
# User hits rate limit in Claude
# Manually notices the error
caam cooldown set claude/work
caam ls claude  # remember what profiles exist
caam activate claude personal
claude "retry my prompt"
```

**After (with this epic complete):**
```bash
claude "do something"
# Rate limit happens automatically handled
# User sees: "⚠️ Rate limit. Switching to 'personal'..."
# Command continues with new profile
```

## Implementation Order
1. **caam-q9f** (Wrapper Mode) - Core capability
2. **caam-bxq** (Init Wizard) - Onboarding experience
3. **caam-k9z** (Shell Integration) - Makes wrapper invisible
4. **caam-hxk** (caam next) - Quick manual switching
5. P2/P3 features in any order

## Measuring Success
- Time to first profile saved: target <2 minutes
- Daily caam commands required: target 0 (shell wrapper handles all)
- Rate limit recovery time: target <5 seconds (automatic)
- User confusion incidents: target 0 (intuitive UX)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-19T19:02:55.725871327-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-19T21:50:19.217733098-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-19T21:50:19.217733098-05:00","close_reason":"All sub-items complete: P0 (wrapper, init), P1 (shell, next), P2 (aliases, workspaces, add, authwatch), P3 (verify, daemon).         })
    }
}

 {"id":"caam-nac","title":"Implement browser launcher abstraction","description":"## Task
Create internal/browser/browser.go with a Launcher interface and implementations for different browsers.

## Design
```go
type Launcher interface {
    // Open opens a URL in the configured browser profile
    Open(url string) error
    // Name returns the browser name for display
    Name() string
}

type Config struct {
    Command    string // Browser executable
    ProfileDir string // Profile directory or name
}
```

## Implementations Needed
1. ChromeLauncher - uses --profile-directory flag
2. FirefoxLauncher - uses -P flag for profile name
3. DefaultLauncher - uses OS default (open/xdg-open)

## Platform Considerations
- macOS: Chrome is at /Applications/Google Chrome.app/Contents/MacOS/Google Chrome
- Linux: Usually 'google-chrome' or 'chromium-browser'
- Windows: Complex paths, need registry lookup or common paths

## Rationale
Abstraction allows us to support multiple browsers and add new ones easily. The interface also makes testing straightforward.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Launcher interface defined
- Chrome implementation works on macOS and Linux
- Firefox implementation works
- Default fallback works
- Unit tests for each implementation","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-17T00:50:26.47883372-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-17T01:16:04.783685539-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-17T01:16:04.783685539-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented browser launcher abstraction with Launcher interface, ChromeLauncher (--profile-directory), FirefoxLauncher (-P), DefaultLauncher (xdg-open/open), and GenericLauncher.  {"id":"caam-nh8","title":"Protected profile naming conventions","description":"Implement special handling for system-managed profiles (prefix with underscore).

## Behavior
- Profiles starting with '_' are 'system profiles' (e.g., _original_claude, _backup_20251217)
- System profiles are created automatically by safety features
- Deleting a system profile requires --force flag
- List command shows system profiles with [system] marker or dimmed

## Profile Types
- `_original_<tool>`: Pre-caam state, created on first activate
- `_backup_<timestamp>`: Auto-backups from smart backup feature

## Implementation
1. Add IsSystemProfile(name) helper in internal/authfile
2. Update Vault.Delete() to check for system profile + require force
3. Update list command to visually distinguish system profiles
4. Add metadata field to meta.json: {type: 'system'|'user', created_by: 'auto'|'user'}
